title: Charmed Life - Chapter 15 (Fragment 4)
text:
Will Suggins turned and marched out of the room. Mary followed him, but she was unable to resist putting her head back around the door to say, “And see how you like that, Eric!” before she shut it.

Cat and Janet looked at one another and then at the lump of dough. “What am I going to do ?” said Cat.

Janet threw her book onto Cat’s bed and tried to pick up the lump of dough. But it had grown to the carpet. She could not shift it. “You’d only get this up by cutting a hole in the floor,” she said. “Cat, this gets worse and worse. If you’ll forgive my saying so, I’ve stopped loving your sugar-coated sister even one tiny bit!”

“It was my fault,” said Cat. “I shouldn’t have lied about Euphemia. That’s what got me in this mess, not Gwendolen.”

“Mess is not a strong enough word,” said Janet. “On Sunday, you get mauled by a tiger. On Monday, it comes out that you can’t do magic. And if the whole story doesn’t come out then, it will on Wednesday, when Mr. Bedlam calls for his money. Do you think Fate has something up its sleeve for Tuesday too? I suppose if you go to meet him on Sunday in the form of yourself, he can’t hurt you much, can he? It’s better than waiting to be turned into a frog.”

“I’d better do that,” Cat agreed, looking at that ominous lump of dough. “I wish I really could turn into things, though. I’d go as a flea. He’d scratch himself to bits trying to find me.”

Janet laughed. “Let’s see if there’s a spell for it.” She turned around to fetch Magic for Beginners and hit her head on the mirror. It was hanging in the air, level with her forehead. “Cat! One of us did it! Look!”

Cat looked, without much interest. He had too much else on his mind. “I expect it was you. You’re the same as Gwendolen, so you’re bound to be able to work spells. But changing into things won’t be in either of those books. That’s Advanced Magic.”

“Then I’ll do the spell to get the mirror down,” said Janet. “Not that I want to be a witch. The more I see of witchcraft, the more it seems just an easy way to be nasty.”

She had opened the book, when there was a knock at the door. Janet seized the chair beside Cat’s bed and stood on it, so as to hide the mirror. Cat hastily dropped to one knee on top of the lump of dough. Neither of them wanted any more trouble.

Janet doubled Magic for Beginners inside out so that it could have been any book, and waved it at Cat. “‘Come into the garden, Maud,’” she proclaimed.

Taking this as an invitation, Miss Bessemer opened the door and came in. She was carrying an armful of things, with a chipped teapot hanging off one finger. “The furnishings I promised you, loves,” she said.

“Oh,” said Janet. “Oh, thanks very much. We were just having a poetry reading, you know.”

“And I made sure you were talking to me!” Miss Bessemer said, laughing. “My name’s Maud. Will these be all right on the bed?”

“Yes, thanks,” said Cat.

Neither of them dared move. They twisted around to watch Miss Bessemer dump the armful on the bed and, still twisted, they thanked her profusely. As soon as Miss Bessemer had gone, they dived to see if, by any blessed chance, any of the pile was valuable. Nothing was. As Janet said, if they really had wanted to play houses, two stools and an old carpet would have been just the thing, but from a selling point of view, they were just a dead loss.

vocabulary.definition: Having small pieces broken off the edge or surface.
vocabulary.example: She was carrying an armful of things, with a chipped teapot hanging off one finger.

vocabulary.definition: Something that is completely useless or unsuccessful.
vocabulary.example: From a selling point of view, they were just a dead loss.

vocabulary.definition: To put down or drop something carelessly or in a mass.
vocabulary.example: They twisted around to watch Miss Bessemer dump the armful on the bed.

vocabulary.definition: To stop feeling angry or resentful towards someone for an offence or mistake.
vocabulary.example: If you’ll forgive my saying so, I’ve stopped loving your sugar-coated sister.

vocabulary.definition: Movable articles necessary for a house or room, such as furniture, curtains, and carpets.
vocabulary.example: The furnishings I promised you, loves.

vocabulary.definition: In a hurried or rushed manner.
vocabulary.example: Cat hastily dropped to one knee on top of the lump of dough.

vocabulary.definition: A request to come somewhere or to do something.
vocabulary.example: Taking this as an invitation, Miss Bessemer opened the door.

vocabulary.definition: To tell an untruth with the intention to deceive.
vocabulary.example: It was my fault, said Cat. I shouldn’t have lied about Euphemia.

vocabulary.definition: A compact mass of a substance, typically one without a definite shape.
vocabulary.example: Cat and Janet looked at one another and then at the lump of dough.

vocabulary.definition: To walk in a deliberate or determined way, often with purpose or anger.
vocabulary.example: Will Suggins turned and marched out of the room.

vocabulary.definition: To attack and injure someone or something by tearing or scratching.
vocabulary.example: On Sunday, you get mauled by a tiger.

vocabulary.definition: A situation that is confusing, difficult, or full of problems.
vocabulary.example: That’s what got me in this mess.

vocabulary.definition: Giving the impression that something bad or unpleasant is going to happen.
vocabulary.example: Cat agreed, looking at that ominous lump of dough.

vocabulary.definition: An event where poems are read aloud aloud for an audience.
vocabulary.example: We were just having a poetry reading.

vocabulary.definition: To announce something officially or publicly.
vocabulary.example: 'Come into the garden, Maud,' she proclaimed.

vocabulary.definition: To a great degree; in large amounts.
vocabulary.example: They thanked her profusely.

vocabulary.definition: To avoid doing something you want to do; to withstand the action or effect of something.
vocabulary.example: Mary followed him, but she was unable to resist putting her head back around the door...

vocabulary.definition: A feature that makes something more attractive to buyers.
vocabulary.example: From a selling point of view, they were just a dead loss.

vocabulary.definition: To move something from one place or position to another.
vocabulary.example: She could not shift it.

vocabulary.definition: A series of words that have magical power when spoken or sung.
vocabulary.example: Janet laughed. “Let’s see if there’s a spell for it.”

vocabulary.definition: Made to appear more pleasant or attractive than it really is.
vocabulary.example: I’ve stopped loving your sugar-coated sister.

vocabulary.definition: Bended out of shape or turned in a distinctive way.
vocabulary.example: They twisted around to watch Miss Bessemer dump the armful.

vocabulary.definition: Worth a lot of money or important in some way.
vocabulary.example: They dived to see if any of the pile was valuable.

vocabulary.definition: The practice of magical skills, spells, and abilities associated with witches.
vocabulary.example: Not that I want to be a witch... The more I see of witchcraft...
